title: Conjecture 1 (Caccetta-Häggkvist Conjecture)
statement_text: Let $r,g\in\mathbb{N}$. Every digraph $D$ with minimum out-degree $\delta^{+}(D)\geq r$ and girth at least $g$ contains at least $r(g-1)+1$ vertices.
context_text: Proposed by Caccetta and Häggkvist in 1978, this is described as one of the most important open problems in extremal graph theory. It remains widely open even for $g=4$. The study of openly disjoint cycles through a vertex was partly motivated by attempts to approach this conjecture.
paper_contribution: The paper proves Mader's Conjecture 4 that $\lim_{r\to\infty}c_r=\infty$ in a strong linear form, a result motivated by attempts to bound $c(D)$ as a route toward this conjecture.
paper_title: Openly disjoint cycles and directed tree-width of regular digraphs

title: Conjecture 2 (Behzad-Chartrand-Wall Conjecture)
statement_text: Let $r,g\in\mathbb{N}$. Every $r$-regular digraph with girth at least $g$ has at least $r(g-1)+1$ vertices.
context_text: Conjectured by Behzad, Chartrand and Wall in 1970, eight years before the Caccetta-Häggkvist conjecture, this is the restriction to regular digraphs and remains open. Seymour's Problem 1 and Mader's Conjecture 4 were both directly motivated by this conjecture.
paper_contribution: The paper proves $c_r\geq\lceil\frac{3}{22}r\rceil$ for all $r$-regular digraphs, establishing linear growth of $c_r$, which was the approach toward proving this conjecture.
paper_title: Openly disjoint cycles and directed tree-width of regular digraphs

title: Conjecture 3 (Hoang-Reed Conjecture)
statement_text: Let $r\in\mathbb{N}$. Every digraph $D$ with $\delta^{+}(D)\geq r$ contains a sequence $C_{1},\ldots,C_{r}$ of directed cycles such that for each $i\in[r]$ it holds that $$\left|V(C_{i})\cap\bigcup_{1\leq j<i}V(C_{j})\right|\leq 1.$$
context_text: This conjecture from 1978 remains open and would directly imply the Caccetta-Häggkvist conjecture. The notion of openly disjoint cycles through a vertex studied in this paper is a particularly simple special case of the cycle sequences required by this conjecture.
paper_contribution: The paper studies openly disjoint cycles through a vertex as a special instance of the cycle sequences in this conjecture, but does not directly prove or disprove the conjecture itself.
notes: The conjecture header in the paper states '1978' but the referenced paper [12] (Hoàng and Reed, 'A note on short cycles in digraphs') was published in 1987; attributed_year reflects the bibliography date.
paper_title: Openly disjoint cycles and directed tree-width of regular digraphs

title: Problem 1 (Seymour's Problem)
context_text: Posed by Seymour in 2005 as a potential route to the Behzad-Chartrand-Wall conjecture. Mader proved the answer is positive for $r=3$ but showed it is negative for $r\geq 8$ by constructing $r$-regular digraphs with $c(D)\leq r-1$; it remains open for $r\in\{4,5,6,7\}$.
paper_contribution: The paper does not directly resolve Problem 1 for the remaining open cases, but proves the related Mader conjecture that $c_r\to\infty$, showing regular digraphs are fundamentally better behaved than general digraphs.
paper_title: Openly disjoint cycles and directed tree-width of regular digraphs

title: Conjecture 4 (Mader's Conjecture)
statement_text: For every $k\in\mathbb{N}$ there exists some $r_{0}\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $c_{r}\geq k$ for every $r\geq r_{0}$. In other words, $\lim_{r\rightarrow\infty}c_{r}=\infty$.
context_text: Conjectured by Mader in 2008 after proving $c_3=3$ and constructing $r$-regular digraphs with $c(D)\leq r-1$ for $r\geq 8$, refuting Seymour's Problem 1. This conjecture is a first main result proved in the paper.
paper_contribution: Proved in strong form: the paper establishes $c_r\geq\lceil\frac{3}{22}r\rceil$ for every $r\in\mathbb{N}$, showing $c_r$ grows linearly in $r$, which is asymptotically best possible.
notes: The conjecture header states '2008' but the referenced paper [23] appears as Mader (2010) in the bibliography.
paper_title: Openly disjoint cycles and directed tree-width of regular digraphs

title: Conjecture 5 (Mader's Subdivision Conjecture)
statement_text: For every acyclic digraph $F$ there exists a number $K=K(F)$ such that every digraph $D$ with $\delta^{+}(D)\geq K$ contains a subdivision of $F$.
context_text: Conjectured by Mader in 1985 as a directed analogue of the Bollobás-Thomason and Komlós-Szemerédi results for undirected graphs. It remains known only for precious few digraphs $F$ such as orientations of paths and cycles, certain orientations of trees, and acyclic digraphs on at most four vertices.
paper_contribution: Corollary 10 shows that subdivisions of digraphs in a significantly richer class of (not necessarily acyclic) digraphs can be guaranteed in regular digraphs, making progress beyond the previously known special cases.
paper_title: Openly disjoint cycles and directed tree-width of regular digraphs

title: Problem 2
context_text: The paper proves that the sequence $(c_r/r)_{r\in\mathbb{N}}$ converges to some limit $L\in[\frac{3}{22},1]$; Problem 2 asks for the exact value of this limit. The problem is stated immediately after the proof of convergence.
paper_title: Openly disjoint cycles and directed tree-width of regular digraphs

title: Informal Question on optimal directed tree-width constant for regular digraphs
statement_text: What is the best-possible constant $\varepsilon>0$ such that every $r$-regular digraph $D$ has directed tree-width at least $\varepsilon r$?
context_text: Motivated by Theorem 7 of the paper, which establishes that every $r$-regular digraph has directed tree-width growing with $r$, the authors pose this question about the optimal linear constant in such a lower bound.
notes: Posed in the text as 'it would be interesting to determine' following Problem 2, without a formal labelled environment.
paper_title: Openly disjoint cycles and directed tree-width of regular digraphs
